# React UX Patterns

Concept-first guidance for designing React components and interactions in this Next.js + TypeScript app (the review dashboard, approval gates, artifact previews). Favor the official mental models over memorized APIs — React's primitives change names faster than its principles do.

## Pull current docs first

React APIs and recommendations shift between releases. Before writing non-trivial component or state logic, fetch the current page rather than relying on training memory:

- Component design: https://react.dev/learn/thinking-in-react
- State structure: https://react.dev/learn/choosing-the-state-structure
- Effects (when NOT to use): https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect
- Pending UI on async actions: https://react.dev/reference/react/useActionState
- Optimistic UI: https://react.dev/reference/react/useOptimistic
- Loading fallbacks: https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense

For Server Components, `"use client"` boundaries, and routing transitions, also confirm against the current Next.js App Router docs — this project is Next.js, and several React loading patterns are surfaced through the framework.

## Core mental models

**Design top-down, derive everything you can.** Break the mockup into a component hierarchy that mirrors the data model (see *Thinking in React*). Then find the *minimal* state: if a value is passed via props, never changes, or can be computed from other state/props, it is **not** state — compute it during render.

**Lift state to the closest common parent** of every component that reads it; push data down via props and changes up via callbacks (one-way data flow + inverse data flow). Don't reach for global state or context until prop-drilling is genuinely painful.

**Structure state to make bad states unrepresentable.** Group values that change together; replace boolean soups (`isSending`/`isSent`) with a single status enum (`'idle' | 'sending' | 'sent' | 'error'`); store IDs not duplicated objects; keep state flat/normalized rather than deeply nested. This maps directly onto the approval-gate UI states (pending / approved / edited / rejected) in this project.

**Effects are an escape hatch for syncing with *external* systems**, not for reacting to your own state. Most `useEffect` calls in a UI are a smell. Use them only for subscriptions, non-React widgets, or browser APIs — and prefer `useSyncExternalStore` for external stores. Data fetching belongs in Server Components / route loaders / actions here, not ad-hoc effects.

**Model async UX with the data-flow primitives, not manual flags:**
- Form submission + pending + returned errors → `useActionState` (return validation errors as state instead of throwing).
- Instant feedback before the server confirms (likes, list inserts, approve/reject clicks) → `useOptimistic`; the setter must run inside an Action or `startTransition`.
- Non-urgent updates that shouldn't block typing/clicks → `useTransition` for an `isPending` flag.
- Declarative loading boundaries → `<Suspense>`, and wrap navigations/updates in a Transition so already-visible content isn't replaced by a jarring fallback flash.

## Checklist

- [ ] Component hierarchy mirrors the data model; each component does roughly one thing.
- [ ] State is minimal — every derivable value is computed in render (`useMemo` only for genuinely expensive work), not stored.
- [ ] No impossible states: related flags collapsed into one status enum.
- [ ] State lives at the lowest common ancestor; data down, events up.
- [ ] No `useEffect` that merely transforms props→state, resets state (use a `key` instead), chains state updates, or fires event logic (move to the handler).
- [ ] Forms use action + `useActionState` for pending/error; mutations show optimistic state where latency is user-visible.
- [ ] Loading handled with Suspense + Transitions, not manual `isLoading` booleans scattered across effects.
- [ ] Lists have stable, identity-based `key`s (never array index for reorderable/editable lists).
- [ ] Interactive elements are real semantic elements (`<button>`, `<label htmlFor>`, focus management on route/gate changes); keyboard + screen-reader paths work — Playwright e2e tests in this repo assert on roles/labels, so author accessible markup.
- [ ] `"use client"` is set only on components that truly need interactivity/state; keep server-rendered surfaces as Server Components.

## Gotchas

- **Effect-to-set-state cascades.** `useEffect(() => setX(derive(props)), [props])` causes an extra render pass and stale flashes. Compute during render, or for the "reset state when a prop changes" case pass `key={id}` to remount instead of an effect.
- **`useEffect` runs twice in dev (Strict Mode).** This is intentional remount-detection, not a bug. Don't "fix" it with a ref guard masking a missing cleanup — write idempotent effects with proper cleanup. One-time app init belongs at module scope or a server boundary, not a bare `[]` effect.
- **Optimistic setter outside a Transition silently does nothing useful.** `useOptimistic`'s setter must be called inside `startTransition` or a form Action; form `action={...}` wraps it automatically, but manual `onClick` handlers do not.
- **Suspense ignores effect/event-handler fetches.** It only triggers on Suspense-enabled sources (framework data fetching, `lazy()`, `use(promise)`). A fallback that never shows usually means the fetch isn't wired into a Suspense-aware source.
- **Fallback flash on already-visible content.** Re-suspending visible content (e.g. re-approving a gate) yanks it for the fallback. Wrap the triggering update in a Transition so React keeps the old UI until the new data is ready.
- **Index-as-key on editable/reorderable lists** (the feature-manifest and artifact lists here) corrupts component state and input focus when items are added/removed/reordered. Use a stable evidence/feature ID.
- **Server/Client boundary mistakes in Next.js:** passing event handlers or non-serializable props from a Server Component into a Client Component fails; `"use client"` at the top of a shared module accidentally pulls a whole subtree to the client. Keep the boundary thin and deliberate.
- **Context as a render-perf tool.** Putting frequently-changing state in a broad Context re-renders every consumer. Split contexts (value vs. dispatch), or keep volatile state local.
- _Project-specific failure modes: none observed yet._
